home.heroDescription: Supporting parents of children with disabilities through community connection, expert information, and early detection resources. You're not alone on this journey.
home.learnDescription: Explore comprehensive information about each disability type, including early signs, diagnosis information, and support resources.
home.featuresDescription: We provide the tools and community support you need to navigate your child's journey with confidence.
home.ctaTitle: Ready to Take the First Step?
home.ctaDescription: Our symptom surveys can help you understand what you're observing and guide you toward the right resources.

disabilities.pageDescription: Understanding your child's condition is the first step toward providing the best support. Select a category below to learn about signs, diagnosis, treatment options, and helpful resources.
disabilities.takeSurveyHint: Take one of our symptom surveys to help identify potential signs and get personalized guidance.

blindness.title: Blindness & Visual Impairment
blindness.description: Learn about visual impairment, early detection signs, diagnosis process, and available support resources.
blindness.shortDesc: Visual impairment resources and early detection signs

deafness.title: Deafness & Hearing Loss
deafness.description: Understand hearing impairment, communication strategies, and how to support your child's development.
deafness.shortDesc: Hearing impairment support and communication strategies

dyslexia.title: Dyslexia
dyslexia.description: Discover how dyslexia affects learning, signs to watch for, and educational support strategies.
dyslexia.shortDesc: Learning difference insights and educational support

speechDisability.title: Speech & Language Disability
speechDisability.description: Explore speech development milestones, common challenges, and therapy options for your child.

survey.pageDescription: Select a category to begin a screening survey. These questionnaires help identify potential signs and guide you toward appropriate next steps. They are not a diagnosis.
survey.disclaimer: These surveys are screening tools only and should not replace professional medical evaluation. If you have concerns about your child, please consult with a healthcare provider.
survey.visionDesc: Check for signs of visual impairment

surveyResults.title: Your Screening Results
surveyResults.lowDescription: Based on your responses, your child shows few of the typical signs we look for. This is reassuring, but continue to monitor their development.
surveyResults.mediumDescription: Your responses indicate some areas worth watching. This doesn't mean there's definitely a concern, but it may be helpful to discuss with a professional.
surveyResults.highDescription: Your responses suggest it would be beneficial to consult with a specialist. Early evaluation can help ensure your child gets any support they might need.
surveyResults.meaningLow: Most children show some of these behaviors occasionally. Your responses suggest your child's development in this area appears typical. However, if you have specific concerns, trust your instincts and speak with your pediatrician.
surveyResults.meaningMedium: Some children develop differently, and that's okay. Your responses suggest it might be helpful to keep an eye on certain areas and consider a professional evaluation to rule out any concerns or get early support if needed.
surveyResults.meaningHigh: Many of the signs you've observed can indicate that professional evaluation would be beneficial. Remember, early identification leads to better outcomes. This is a positive step toward supporting your child.
surveyResults.step1Monitor: Continue to observe and note any changes in your child's behavior
surveyResults.step1Discuss: Schedule an appointment with a specialist to discuss your observations
surveyResults.step2Low: Schedule regular check-ups as recommended for your child's age
surveyResults.step2Medium: Consider scheduling a professional evaluation within the next few months
surveyResults.step2High: Reach out to your pediatrician this week to discuss your concerns
surveyResults.step3: Document specific behaviors to share with healthcare providers
surveyResults.step4: Explore the resources below to learn more about available support
surveyResults.encouragementText: Taking this survey shows you care deeply about your child's wellbeing. Remember, this is a screening tool to help guide you—not a diagnosis. Whatever the results, you're taking positive steps for your child.

resources.pageDescription: Discover specialists, therapists, and support services near you to help your child thrive.
resources.searchPlaceholder: Search by name, description, or city...
resources.noResultsHint: Try adjusting your filters or search query to find more results.

surveys.title: Symptom Surveys
surveys.description: Interactive questionnaires to help identify early signs and get personalized guidance

community.title: Parent Community
community.description: Connect with other parents who understand your journey through forums and messaging

resources.title: Supportive Resources
resources.description: Curated information about treatments, therapies, and local support services

about.title: About Us
about.description: Learn more about Safe Steps and our mission to support families.
about.missionDesc: Safe Steps is dedicated to empowering parents and caregivers of children with disabilities. We believe every family deserves access to knowledge, community support, and local resources to help their children thrive.
about.whatWeDoDesc: We provide screening surveys to help identify early signs of disabilities, connect parents through our community forum, and curate a directory of verified local resources including schools, therapists, and support organizations across Kerala.
about.contactDesc: Have questions or suggestions? We'd love to hear from you. Reach out to us at safesteps@example.com and our team will get back to you as soon as possible.

footer.description: Supporting parents of children with disabilities through community connection and resources.

blindness.title: Vision Screening Survey
blindness.description: This survey helps identify potential visual impairment signs in children. It is not a diagnosis but can help guide you toward professional evaluation.

b1.question: Does your child have difficulty tracking moving objects with their eyes?
b1.description: For example, following a toy or your face as it moves side to side

b2.question: Does your child frequently bump into furniture or objects?
b2.description: More than what seems typical for their age

b3.question: Does your child hold books or objects very close to their face?

b4.question: Does your child squint or close one eye when looking at things?

b5.question: Does your child tilt their head to see better?

b6.question: Does your child complain of headaches, especially after visual tasks?
b6.description: Reading, screen time, or detailed work

b7.question: Does your child have difficulty recognizing familiar faces from a distance?

b8.question: Does your child avoid activities that require good vision?
b8.description: Drawing, puzzles, reading, or ball games

b9.question: Have you noticed your child's eyes appearing misaligned or crossed?

b10.question: Does your child have unusual sensitivity to light?

deafness.title: Hearing Screening Survey
deafness.description: This survey helps identify potential hearing impairment signs in children. Early detection is crucial for speech and language development.

d1.question: Does your child respond when you call their name from another room?

d2.question: Does your child turn toward sounds or voices?

d3.question: Does your child ask 'What?' or need things repeated frequently?

d4.question: Does your child speak louder than seems necessary?

d5.question: Does your child prefer the TV or music at a louder volume than others?

d6.question: Does your child watch your face intently when you speak?

d7.question: Has your child's speech development seemed delayed compared to peers?

d8.question: Does your child have difficulty following verbal instructions?

d9.question: Does your child seem to hear better from one ear than the other?

d10.question: Has your child had frequent ear infections?

dyslexia.title: Learning Difference Screening Survey
dyslexia.description: This survey helps identify potential signs of dyslexia in children. Dyslexia affects reading and language processing but is not related to intelligence.

dy1.question: Does your child have difficulty learning letter names and sounds?

dy2.question: Does your child confuse letters that look similar (b/d, p/q)?

dy3.question: Does your child struggle to sound out new words?

dy4.question: Does your child read very slowly compared to classmates?

dy5.question: Does your child avoid reading aloud or get anxious about it?

dy6.question: Does your child have difficulty spelling common words?
dy6.description: Words they should know by now

dy7.question: Does your child have trouble remembering sequences (days, months, alphabet)?

dy8.question: Does your child have difficulty following written instructions?
dy8.description: Even when they understand verbal instructions well

dy9.question: Does your child skip words or lines when reading?

dy10.question: Is there a family history of reading or spelling difficulties?

speech_disability.title: Speech & Language Screening Survey
speech_disability.description: This survey helps identify potential speech and language delays or disorders in children. Early intervention can significantly improve outcomes.

s1.question: Is your child's speech difficult for others to understand?

s2.question: Does your child substitute sounds in words (e.g., 'wabbit' for 'rabbit')?

s3.question: Does your child stutter or repeat sounds/words?

s4.question: Does your child have difficulty putting words together in sentences?

s5.question: Does your child have a limited vocabulary for their age?

s6.question: Does your child have trouble finding the right words to express themselves?

s7.question: Does your child have difficulty following conversations?

s8.question: Does your child avoid speaking in social situations?

s9.question: Has your child been late in reaching speech milestones?

s10.question: Does your child have difficulty with the rhythm or flow of speech?
s10.description: Speaking too fast, too slow, or monotone

low: Continue monitoring your child's visual development
Schedule regular eye check-ups as recommended for their age
Ensure good lighting when your child reads or does detailed work
medium: Schedule an appointment with a pediatric ophthalmologist
Talk to your child's teacher about their classroom vision
Consider a comprehensive eye exam in the next few weeks
Monitor for any changes in visual behaviors
high: We strongly recommend scheduling an appointment with a pediatric ophthalmologist soon
Talk to your pediatrician about your observations
Request a vision screening at school if available
Keep a log of visual behaviors to share with the doctor
Learn about resources for children with visual impairments

low: Continue monitoring your child's hearing and speech development
Ensure regular hearing screenings at well-child visits
Maintain a language-rich environment at home
medium: Schedule a hearing evaluation with an audiologist
Discuss your observations with your pediatrician
Monitor for any changes in hearing behaviors
Consider having hearing checked in different environments
high: We strongly recommend scheduling a comprehensive hearing evaluation with a pediatric audiologist
Talk to your pediatrician about your concerns right away
Learn about early intervention services in your area
Consider meeting with a speech-language pathologist
Explore resources for children with hearing impairments

low: Continue supporting your child's reading development
Read together daily and make it enjoyable
Monitor progress and keep communication open with teachers
medium: Request an educational evaluation through your child's school
Talk to the teacher about specific reading challenges observed
Consider additional reading support or tutoring
high: Request a formal evaluation for learning disabilities through the school
Consider a private educational psychologist evaluation
Research Orton-Gillingham or other structured literacy approaches
Connect with the school's special education team
Join support groups for parents of children with dyslexia

low: Read and talk with your child regularly
medium: Schedule an evaluation with a speech-language pathologist
Practice speech exercises recommended by professionals
Be patient and give your child time to express themselves
high: We strongly recommend evaluation by a speech-language pathologist
Talk to your pediatrician about early intervention services
Learn about speech therapy options in your area
Create a supportive environment for communication at home
Connect with other parents of children with speech differences

blindness.title: Blindness & Visual Impairment
blindness.description: Understanding visual impairment in children, from early detection to support strategies

overview.definition: Visual impairment in children ranges from partial sight loss to complete blindness. It affects how children learn, develop, and interact with their environment. Early identification and intervention are crucial for optimal development.
overview.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 1,000 children are born with significant visual impairment. An additional 1 in 3,000 children become visually impaired before age 16.
keyPoints: Vision develops rapidly in the first years of life
Many visual impairments can be corrected or managed if caught early
Children can thrive with proper support and accommodations
Early intervention services can significantly improve outcomes

infants: Doesn't make eye contact by 3 months
Eyes appear crossed or turned out after 4 months
White or grayish-white color in the pupil
Eyes that flutter quickly from side to side or up and down
toddlers: Holds objects very close to face
Squints or closes one eye frequently
Tilts head to look at things
Bumps into objects often
Shows sensitivity to light
Rubs eyes frequently
schoolAge: Difficulty reading or loses place while reading
Sits very close to TV or computer
Trouble seeing the board at school

diagnosis.process: Diagnosis typically involves comprehensive eye examinations that assess visual acuity, eye alignment, and overall eye health. Pediatricians often perform initial screenings, with referrals to ophthalmologists for detailed evaluation.
diagnosis.timeline: Initial screenings begin at birth and continue through childhood. If concerns arise, specialized testing can be performed at any age, with some tests adapted for infants.

options.title: Corrective Lenses
options.description: Glasses or contact lenses can correct refractive errors like nearsightedness, farsightedness, and astigmatism.

options.title: Surgery
options.description: Surgical procedures can address conditions like cataracts, strabismus (crossed eyes), or blocked tear ducts.

options.title: Vision Therapy
options.description: Structured exercises and activities to improve visual skills and processing.

options.title: Low Vision Aids
options.description: Magnifiers, large print materials, and assistive technology for children with partial sight.

options.title: Orientation & Mobility Training
options.description: Teaching children to navigate their environment safely and independently.

treatment.earlyIntervention: Early intervention services for visually impaired children can begin as early as birth. These programs help develop compensatory skills and support overall development.

resources.title: American Foundation for the Blind
resources.description: Comprehensive resources for families of children with visual impairments.

resources.title: National Federation of the Blind
resources.description: Advocacy, resources, and community support for blind individuals and families.

resources.title: Lighthouse Guild
resources.description: Vision rehabilitation services and support programs.

resources.title: Family Connect
resources.description: Online community and resources specifically for parents of visually impaired children.

faqs.question: Can my child attend regular school?
faqs.answer: Yes! Many children with visual impairments attend mainstream schools with appropriate accommodations. These may include large print materials, preferential seating, assistive technology, or support from a vision teacher.

faqs.question: Will my child need to learn Braille?
faqs.answer: Not all visually impaired children need Braille. The decision depends on the degree of vision loss and whether it's progressive. A vision specialist can help determine the best literacy approach.

faqs.question: How can I help my child at home?
faqs.answer: Create a consistent environment, use verbal descriptions, encourage independence, and work with vision specialists to learn specific strategies for daily activities.

deafness.title: Deafness & Hearing Loss
deafness.description: Comprehensive guide to hearing impairment in children, communication strategies, and support

overview.definition: Hearing loss in children can range from mild to profound and may be present at birth (congenital) or develop later (acquired). It affects language development, communication, and social interaction.
overview.prevalence: About 2-3 of every 1,000 children are born with detectable hearing loss. This number increases with age as some children develop hearing loss later.
keyPoints: Newborn hearing screening catches most congenital hearing loss
Early identification (by 3 months) and intervention (by 6 months) are critical
Many children with hearing loss develop age-appropriate language with proper support
Technology and communication strategies continue to improve outcomes

infants: Doesn't turn toward sounds by 6 months
Seems to hear some sounds but not others
Is not soothed by your voice
toddlers: Delayed speech development
Unclear speech compared to peers
Needs TV or music louder than others
Doesn't follow simple directions
Says 'huh?' or 'what?' frequently
Watches faces intently when being spoken to
schoolAge: Complains of earaches or ringing in ears

diagnosis.process: Diagnosis begins with newborn hearing screening. If concerns arise, audiologists perform comprehensive hearing evaluations. Testing is adapted for the child's age and development level.
diagnosis.timeline: Newborn screening occurs before hospital discharge. If screening isn't passed, diagnostic testing should occur by 3 months. Intervention should begin by 6 months.

options.title: Hearing Aids
options.description: Devices that amplify sound for children with mild to severe hearing loss.

options.title: Cochlear Implants
options.description: Surgically implanted devices that provide sound perception for severe to profound hearing loss.

options.title: Speech-Language Therapy
options.description: Specialized therapy to develop speech, language, and listening skills.

options.title: Sign Language
options.description: Visual language that can be used alone or alongside spoken language (total communication).

options.title: FM Systems
options.description: Wireless technology that transmits the speaker's voice directly to hearing aids.

treatment.earlyIntervention: Early intervention for deaf and hard-of-hearing children focuses on language development, whether through spoken language, sign language, or both. Services often include family support and parent education.

resources.title: National Association of the Deaf
resources.description: Civil rights organization providing resources and advocacy for deaf individuals.

resources.title: Hands & Voices
resources.description: Parent-driven organization supporting families of deaf and hard-of-hearing children.

resources.title: AG Bell Association
resources.description: Resources for families pursuing spoken language outcomes.

resources.title: Gallaudet University
resources.description: Leading academic institution for deaf education and resources.

faqs.question: Should my child learn sign language?
faqs.answer: This is a personal family decision. Many families choose spoken language only, sign language only, or a combination. Consider your family's communication needs, resources, and your child's specific hearing loss.

faqs.question: Will my child be able to speak?
faqs.answer: Many children with hearing loss develop spoken language, especially with early identification and intervention. Factors include degree of hearing loss, age of intervention, and type of amplification used.

faqs.question: How do I communicate with my baby before they have language?
faqs.answer: Use visual communication (facial expressions, gestures), maintain eye contact, and consider learning baby sign language. Your audiologist and early intervention team can provide specific strategies.

dyslexia.title: Dyslexia
dyslexia.description: Understanding dyslexia: signs, assessment, and effective strategies for supporting your child

overview.definition: Dyslexia is a specific learning disability that affects reading, writing, and spelling. It's neurological in origin and is not related to intelligence. Children with dyslexia often have average to above-average intelligence.
overview.prevalence: Dyslexia affects approximately 15-20% of the population to some degree. It's the most common learning disability, accounting for 80% of all learning disabilities.
keyPoints: Dyslexia is a lifelong condition, but with support, individuals can excel
It often runs in families (genetic component)
Early identification and structured literacy instruction are key
Many successful individuals have dyslexia, including entrepreneurs and artists

toddlers: Trouble learning letter names and sounds
Difficulty recognizing own name in print
Avoiding drawing or writing activities
Struggling to retell a story in order
Mixing up words that sound similar
Difficulty clapping out syllables in words

diagnosis.process: Dyslexia diagnosis involves comprehensive evaluation of reading skills, language abilities, cognitive processing, and academic achievement. Testing looks for the specific patterns associated with dyslexia.
diagnosis.timeline: While signs can appear in preschool, formal diagnosis typically occurs in elementary school when reading instruction begins. However, many individuals aren't diagnosed until adolescence or adulthood.

options.title: Structured Literacy Instruction
options.description: Systematic, explicit teaching of phonics, phonemic awareness, and decoding skills (like Orton-Gillingham approach).

options.title: Reading Intervention Programs
options.description: Evidence-based programs like Wilson Reading System, Lindamood-Bell, or Barton Reading.

options.title: Accommodations
options.description: Extended time on tests, audiobooks, speech-to-text software, and alternative assessment methods.

options.title: Assistive Technology
options.description: Text-to-speech software, digital highlighters, and organizational apps.

options.title: Educational Therapy
options.description: One-on-one work with a specialist to develop reading and learning strategies.

treatment.earlyIntervention: Early intervention for dyslexia focuses on building phonological awareness and letter-sound knowledge before formal reading instruction begins. Pre-K programs can identify at-risk children.

resources.title: International Dyslexia Association
resources.description: Leading organization providing information, research, and resources about dyslexia.

resources.title: Learning Ally
resources.description: Audiobook library and support for students with dyslexia.

resources.title: Understood.org
resources.description: Comprehensive resources for parents of children with learning differences.

resources.title: Bookshare
resources.description: Accessible ebook library for students with reading barriers.

faqs.question: Does dyslexia mean my child sees letters backwards?
faqs.answer: No, this is a common misconception. Dyslexia is primarily about difficulty processing the sounds in language (phonological processing), not visual processing. Letter reversals are common in all young children learning to write.

faqs.question: Will my child ever be able to read well?
faqs.answer: With appropriate instruction and support, most children with dyslexia can become proficient readers. They may always read more slowly, but can develop strong comprehension and enjoyment of reading.

faqs.question: Is dyslexia related to intelligence?
faqs.answer: Absolutely not! Dyslexia occurs across all levels of intelligence. Many highly intelligent and successful people have dyslexia.

speech-disability.title: Speech & Language Disorders
speech-disability.description: Guide to speech and language disorders in children: understanding, identification, and support

overview.definition: Speech and language disorders encompass difficulties with producing speech sounds, fluency (stuttering), voice, and/or understanding and using language. These disorders can affect communication, learning, and social interaction.
overview.prevalence: Approximately 8-9% of young children have a speech or language disorder. Many of these are successfully treated with early intervention.
keyPoints: Speech disorders affect how sounds are produced
Language disorders affect understanding or expressing ideas
Many children have both speech and language difficulties
Early intervention leads to the best outcomes

infants: Doesn't respond to sounds or voices
Not using any words by 12 months
Doesn't seem to understand simple words
toddlers: Using fewer than 50 words by age 2
Not combining words by age 2
Speech difficult for parents to understand
Losing words they previously used
Frustration with communication
Not following simple directions
schoolAge: Difficulty being understood by unfamiliar listeners
Struggling to tell stories in order

diagnosis.process: Speech-language pathologists conduct comprehensive evaluations examining all aspects of communication including speech sound production, language comprehension and expression, fluency, and social communication.
diagnosis.timeline: Concerns can be evaluated at any age. Many speech-language milestones occur in the first three years, so early evaluation is encouraged if parents have concerns.

options.title: Speech Therapy
options.description: Individual or group sessions focusing on specific speech or language goals.

options.title: Articulation Therapy
options.description: Practice producing specific speech sounds correctly.

options.title: Language Intervention
options.description: Activities to build vocabulary, grammar, and conversation skills.

options.title: Fluency Therapy
options.description: Techniques to manage stuttering and improve smooth speech.

options.title: Augmentative Communication (AAC)
options.description: Communication devices or systems for children who need support expressing themselves.

treatment.earlyIntervention: Early intervention for speech-language delays begins as early as birth. Services often include parent coaching to support communication development in daily routines.

resources.title: American Speech-Language-Hearing Association
resources.description: Professional organization with family resources and therapist finder.

resources.title: The Stuttering Foundation
resources.description: Resources and support specifically for families dealing with stuttering.

resources.title: Late Talkers Foundation
resources.description: Support for families of children with language delays.

resources.title: NIDCD - Speech and Language
resources.description: National Institutes of Health information on speech-language disorders.

faqs.question: Is it normal for toddlers to be hard to understand?
faqs.answer: Some unclear speech is normal in toddlers. However, by age 2, parents should understand about 50% of their child's speech, and by age 3, about 75%. Strangers typically understand most of a 4-year-old's speech.

faqs.question: Will my child grow out of it?
faqs.answer: Some children do catch up on their own, but there's no way to predict which children will. Early evaluation and intervention are recommended because treatment is most effective when started early.

faqs.question: Does using a pacifier or bottle cause speech problems?
faqs.answer: Extended pacifier or bottle use can affect oral development, but these are rarely the sole cause of speech problems. If you have concerns, discuss them with your pediatrician or a speech-language pathologist.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.description: Find answers to common questions about Safe Steps and our services.
faq.a1: Safe Steps is a platform designed to help parents identify early signs of disabilities in children and connect them with supportive resources and a caring community.
faq.q2: Are the symptom surveys a medical diagnosis?
faq.a2: No. Our surveys are screening tools designed to help identify potential signs. They are not a substitute for professional medical evaluation. We always recommend consulting a healthcare professional for a proper diagnosis.
faq.a3: Yes. We take your privacy very seriously. All survey responses and personal information are securely stored and never shared with third parties without your consent.
faq.q4: How can I connect with other parents?
faq.a4: You can join our Community Forum where parents share experiences, ask questions, and support each other. Simply create an account and start participating in discussions.
faq.q5: What types of disabilities does Safe Steps cover?
faq.a5: Currently, Safe Steps focuses on four areas: Blindness/Visual Impairment, Deafness/Hearing Loss, Dyslexia/Learning Disabilities, and Speech Disabilities.
faq.q6: How do I find local resources near me?
faq.a6: Visit our Resources page where you can search for verified local support services, therapists, schools, and organizations filtered by your location and disability type.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.description: How we collect, use, and protect your information.
privacy.dataCollectionDesc: We collect information you provide when creating an account, taking surveys, and participating in community discussions. This includes your name, email address, survey responses, and forum posts.
privacy.dataUsageDesc: Your data is used to provide personalized recommendations, connect you with relevant resources, and improve our services. Survey data may be used in aggregate (anonymized) form for research purposes.
privacy.dataSecurityDesc: We implement industry-standard security measures to protect your personal information. All data is encrypted in transit and at rest. We regularly review our security practices to ensure your data remains safe.
privacy.yourRightsDesc: You have the right to access, modify, or delete your personal data at any time. You can also request a copy of all data we hold about you. Contact us to exercise any of these rights.
privacy.contactDesc: If you have any questions about our privacy practices or wish to exercise your data rights, please reach out to us through our community forum or email us at support@safesteps.org.
